title: Lesson 389
transcript: We just finished a section on transport architecture. Of course, we're in the architecture section of domain number 4, which is network security. Here's a perfect example of where we are completely aligned with the exam outline. But when you look at the actual exam outline from ISC2, specifically for the CISSP, it doesn't flow perfectly. In fact, the exam outline hasn't been written so that things make sense from the perspective of the order that they appear in. And here's a perfect example of that, because in the previous section, we talked about transport architecture and we had to mention words like topology, which is what we're going to see in this section. All right. So there's a bit of a flow problem, but it's not intentional, I suppose. because that's the way things appear in the exam outline. And because we want to be completely aligned with the exam outline, this is the way that we want to present things. But be assured that at the end of all of this, Everything fits together. The pieces of the puzzle will fit together nicely for you, all right? But let's talk about this next section that again comes directly from the exam outline. which is focused on network topologies. And again, I think if you look at the word topology, that's the key word here. A network topology is how we physically and logically connect devices together. And so what you're going to see on this slide are the five most commonly used network topologies. And of course, we have to look at them from a perspective of security. Everything has to be related to security. This is a security exam. And therefore, when we look at especially very technical things, we have to try and relate it to security. But always look at it from the perspective of management, for example. So by far the most commonly used and implemented network topology today is what's called bus. And the reason it's called bus is because all of your devices, as you can see here, are connected to a central bus. And the bus is essentially a wire. So imagine one long wire running through a building, for example, all of your devices are going to be connected to that wire. That's essentially what bus topology looks like. Now, the reason this is very popular and the foundation really for all networking today is because it's very scalable, right? I mean, if we wanted to add more devices to the network, we simply extend the bus. It's very easy to do. The only problem with that is what we call attenuation, which means loss of signal strength over distance, but we can handle that very easily with boosters and repeaters for example so that's not actually a problem at all all right so it's very scalable and in fact you know by how we implement this this can be a very efficient and scalable type of network topology and why it's the foundation for really all networking today. And the other reason why this is the foundation for all networking today is because you're going to see when we go through the others, that this supports all of these others. So typically most networks today are going to be essentially bus for its foundation, but then you're going to have other topologies that you're going to see here. basically for functionality, but also for security. That's what we have to keep in mind. All right. Now, because we have to relate this to security, we obviously have to think about the security advantages and disadvantages of something like bus. All right. So when you look at connecting everything to one central bus, the bus itself or the wire itself becomes a single point of failure. And so we have to address that. We have to make it redundant. And implementations of bus topologies can certainly do that very easily. Now, some of the other problems that you might see here are things like, for example, collisions. What happens if two devices need to send information and they do it at the same time? Well, a collision is going to happen, which means none of that information is going to get to where it's supposed to. So we have to be able to handle collisions because essentially only one bit can be carried across that wire at any given point in time. So if you have two devices that send information at the same time, It's going to result in collisions. We have to be able to handle collisions. The other obvious problem here is lack of confidentiality. Because look, everybody is connected to that same bus, that same wire. So if someone's sending information, who has opportunity to look at that information? Well, the answer to that is everyone. Because we're all connected to the bus, the wire that information has to go across. We all have the opportunity to see it. So this obviously does not provide inherently confidentiality. So if we want confidentiality, we have to achieve it using other methods. But there are some good things about this. We already talked about obviously scalability, but it also permits what's called node failure. So any of these nodes can go down. That doesn't really impact the rest of the network. The rest of the devices can still communicate and do things across that network, even though some of these devices may have gone down. So we call that it permits node failure. Here's the next topology that we need to look at, and it's called tree. And they've done a pretty good job of obviously naming these for what they typically look like. They've done a pretty good job of naming these properly. So tree topology looks like something like this. You can start branching down. so that you can limit or restrict the traffic to go down a certain branch. And you can see an immediate advantage of that from a security perspective, because if we force the traffic to go down a certain branch like this one, These devices over here will not have an opportunity to see that traffic. So we can start again implementing bus topology as the foundation. But now we can start adding, for example, segments that may be tree based so that we can start doing things related to security. and also functionality, all right? Here's the next topology that we need to look at. And again, appropriately called, it's called star topology because every device is connected to a central other device, like a switch or a hub. And so obviously what you see here, first of all, is a single point of failure is going to be the central device itself. So we need to, again, address redundancy because redundancy is the way that we address single point of failure, cost effective redundancy. But obviously, by utilizing, for example, switches, which we're going to talk about later, this gives us the opportunity to start taking advantage of what star topology really gives us because we can connect devices to different ports. at the back of that switch and we can limit for example traffic to go out to certain ports so that now we can start creating really what are called virtual lands and again we'll talk about these things later on as well. Now, it was hard for us to show this, but again, this is why bus topology is the foundation for everything because it supports all of these other topologies that you see here. We could have easily taken for example this entire star diagram that you see here and place it up here as part of bus which is essentially what all networks do right networks are essentially bus but they support all of these other topologies not only for just functionality but also for security right here's the next one that we need to mention And I think this one's going to be pretty easy to reflect on from a security perspective, and that is mesh topology. Mesh simply means that every device is connected to every other device specifically. That's what they refer to as full mesh topology. although most organizations will implement what's called partial mesh topology where your most critical devices are connected to your most other critical devices. And obviously what this offers as a benefit or advantage from a security perspective would be redundancy, because every device is connected to every other device specifically. So that gives us really good availability and redundancy and so on. mesh topology is what this is. And the last one that we're going to look at, again, appropriately called ring topology. And you may have heard terminology in the past, like IBM token ring. which would have been an implementation of ring topology, where you still connect devices by using wires, obviously, but it's a closed loop. Obviously wouldn't be as scalable as something like bus here because you can't really stretch the ring. So you're limited, for example, in how many devices you can connect. And the more devices you can connect, the less efficient this type of network topology becomes. Obviously, it's not as scalable, it's not as simple to add more devices because you've got restrictions based on the size of that loop, if you will. What this shows is a benefit of ring topology, if you look at the little animation that we've got here, and that is something called a token. A token is passed from device to device, and that really dictates the priority on who can send information so the idea is this gets rid of the problem of collisions because if you want to send information you have to wait for that token to get to you But in the same token, it's not as efficient, is it? Because if you've got a large network and you're waiting to send information, you have to wait for that token to get to you, which might take a while, depending on the size of the network itself. So it's not as efficient. as, for example, bus topology or some of these other ones that you see here. And in fact, there's another problem related to that and really why this has disappeared. But what happens if a device goes down? Now we don't have the opportunity of passing that token from this device over to this device. So one node going down may affect the entire network. And obviously that's not a good thing. We can maybe take care of things by, for example, reversing the ring. or we can add a secondary ring for redundancy purposes but it's not as efficient as for example bus all right so these are really the five most commonly implemented topologies and we looked at them from a security perspective which is obviously what you need to focus on all right Now, related to this, there's two ways to really connect devices together, and that's the difference between what you see here. I think the words used is self-explanatory, and it tells you exactly what we're talking about here. Topology, again, means how we map things together, how we physically and logically connect devices together. And that's what this slide is talking about. We can physically connect devices together that is referred to as physical topology. So we physically link devices together using physical connections and physical constructs. And logical topology really defines logical. You can think of software as being logical, for example. We can define how things are connected together and therefore how they share and send information and how data flows across a network from a logical perspective. Regardless of its physical layout, we can logically structure it based on some of those topologies that we just looked at before. That's the difference. Just focus on the words physical, physically linked, logical, linked by software. logically linked together and how data flows across those logical connections. Now, I think we introduced these earlier. but they are specifically mentioned in the exam outline. We'll just review these and look at these specifically. We talked about the difference logical constructs that all network devices really need to be logically divided into. We talked about the data plane as being the doer, right? It is the part of the device, like we used, I think earlier switches as an example, but these could be routers as well. Any network device ultimately will have a data plane that actually does the work. It forwards the packets or the frames based on the decisions that were made by the control plane. If you focus on the words control and data plane here. The control plane makes the decisions and then it passes that decision down to the data plane that actually forwards the traffic based on the decisions that were made by the control plane. So any device out there, network device, really will have these two constructs separated. The control plane will make the decisions, but it's the data plane that will actually move the packets and the information based on the decisions that were made by the control plane and of course we can take this to another level as well right and this is where later we're going to talk about software defined networking Right. And if you think about those words, software defined networking, it's networking that is defined through software. So imagine a centralized application that manages everything that the network does. So it manages the entire network and its devices and its constructs and how we logically and physically take care of for example the topologies but also how security is handled throughout the entire network itself is something that we can centrally manage through centralized software that is really what we call the management plane right so these are the constructs that really allow us to move information across networks, regardless of what the topology looks like. And so I think earlier we looked at, you know, ultimately the data plane is what moves the information, right? It's the low level component in a device that actually forwards the packets or the frames based on the decisions that were made by the control plane, all right? But there's two ways that this can be done. and we introduced these earlier, but important to focus on some of the key words here. So the data plane can ultimately do switching of information using cut through switching and store and forward switching. And if you think about these words, it tells you the difference. In cut through switching, we can start forwarding the packet or the frame as soon as we understand the destination address. As soon as we receive a few bytes of information, the switch can look at the destination address and simply start forwarding it, even though it hasn't received the entire packet or frame. This is more efficient. But as it says here, does not allow for error checking because we haven't received the entire packet or frame. All right. So this is more efficient, but doesn't do anything related to error checking. Whereas store and forward is actually the opposite. We have to wait for the entire packet or frame to be received, and then we do some error checking, and then we forward that information based on the receiving address or the destination address that we have. So cut through starts right away as soon as we understand the address, but does not do anything related to error correction. Whereas store and forward, because we have to wait for the entire thing to be received and stored, we can do some error checking and then forward the packet, which obviously means this introduces more latency because we have to wait to receive the entire thing before we can check it for errors and forward it to its destination. All right, so if you look at it from a security perspective, this is faster but doesn't do anything related to the integrity of the transmission. This is slower but does offer us some error correction capabilities.
